rulings.Case Title: C.I. ENERGIA SOLAR S.A., INC., VS. BAGATELOS GLASS SYSTEMS, INC., ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Service Of Summons
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday July 2, 2019, Line 2, CROSS DEFENDANT ESWINDOWS, LLC'S Motion To Quash Service Of Summons Cross-defendant ESWindows, LLC's motion to quash service of summons for lack of personal jurisdiction is denied. The Court finds that ESWindows had sufficient minimum contacts with California for purposes of its exercising specific jurisdiction over it for Cross-Complainant Bagatelos Glass Systems, Inc.'s claims. ESWindows directed its activities to the forum state by contracting with or at least performing a major part of the contract with Bagatelos. In particular, ESWindows provided the $1,500,938 written quote to Bagatelos "for ESWindows LLC to supply" the window and curtain wall systems (Rodriguez Decl. Exh. G; Archila Depo. 27-33); ESWindows' employees prepared specific designs for the project and were involved in their installation, including the project-specific shop drawings (Rodriguez Decl. J; Archila Depo. 75); the project manager for the project was an employee of ESWindows (Chamorro Depo. 35; Amin Depo. 110-11); the project was "a project of ESWindows, LLC" on which ESWindows employees worked (Archilla Depo. 31-32, 46-47; Subeh Depo. 19, 28-29), including participating in an on-site visit (Archila Depo. 210-213) and directing third parties working onsite (Subeh Depo. 28-29; 76-77); and ESWindows was involved with the booklet for the project (Vinas Depo Depo. 25-29; Chamorro Depo. 17-19). All of Bagatelos' claims arise from ESWindows' conduct related to the project located in California, and ESWindows has not shown that exercise of the court's jurisdiction over it for the alleged acts of its employees directed at or in the forum state would be unreasonable. (See Luberski, Inc v. Oleificio F.lli Amato S.R.L. (2009) 171 Cal.App.4th 409, 419 [court had specific jurisdiction over Italian company that purposefully availed itself of California forum by contracting to deliver goods in California]; Magnecomp Corp. v. Athene Co. (1989) 209 Cal.App.3d 526, 535 ["Since corporations by their nature can only operate through their appointed agents, courts consistently exercise personal jurisdiction over corporations based upon their agents' activities within the forum state."].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Bagatelos is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: REINA CRUZ CANTE VS. GERALDINE ANNE SCOTT ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Amended Notice Of Motion And Motion For Protective Order To Seal Records And Proof Of Service
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday July 2, 2019, Line 12, DEFENDANT GERRIE SCOTT'S Motion For Protective Order To Seal Records Defendant Gerrie Scott's motion for protective order to seal records is denied. Defendant requests the Court to order that "the entire case record, enumerated by the Register of Actions," be sealed. However, Defendant fails to show that any of the criteria set forth in Rule 2.550(d) of the California Rules of Court are met as to any individual record in the court file, much less the entire file. In particular, she fails even to articulate any overriding privacy or other interest in any record filed in this case that overcomes the right of public access to the Court's records. Defendant's assertions that "there was a negotiated settlement agreement between the parties in this case which provided for sealing and confidentiality, and that out-of-court settlement precluded the need for a trial," are patently inadequate to satisfy the criteria of the Rule. (See, e.g., Huffy Corp. v. Superior Court (2004) 112 Cal.App.4th 97, 105-107 [reversing trial court's order sealing documents including settlement agreement with non-party which had a confidentiality provision; "before papers can be sealed, there must be substantial prejudice to an overriding interest if the documents are made available to the public."].) The mere fact that Defendant objects to having been named in a lawsuit, or would prefer that the public not be aware of that fact, is not grounds for sealing the court's file. Defendant's arguments that Rule 2.550 does not apply and, in the alternative, that her "penumbra right to privacy outweighs the public's right to know under the 1st Amendment" are meritless. Finally, unless they are attached to records in the court's file, the deposition transcripts and responses to discovery requests Defendant refers to are in the parties' possession, not the court's, and therefore cannot be "sealed" by the Court. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JACK H REISNER VS. UNITED BROTHERHOOD OF CARPENTERS AND JOINERS OF AM ET AL

Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Continued to July 11, 2019 per the agreement of the parties (email dated 06/28/19). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@sbslawsf.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: KIMBERLY JOY HOM VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To Take Second Physical Examination Filed By Defendant San Francisco Zoological Society
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, July 02, 2019, Line 2, DEFENDANT SAN FRANCISCO ZOOLOGICAL SOCIETY's Motion For Leave To Take Second Physical Examination Filed By Defendant San Francisco Zoological Society. Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendant's request for a second medical examination by an orthopedic surgeon is granted. Plaintiff alleges an accident related head and left lower extremity injury. The parties previously agreed to a physical examination by a neurologist who also examined plaintiff's left lower extremity. Good cause exists for a second examination by an orthopedist because of the significant claimed injuries to plaintiff's left lower extremity with a related surgical procedure. If the parties had not agreed to an examination by an orthopedic specialist at the time a physical examination was initially discussed the Court would have granted same. Plaintiff's request to describe the orthopedic examination as a "Defense Medical Exam" or limit the neurologist's trial testimony referable to examination of plaintiff's left lower extremity is referred to the discretion of the Trial Judge. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@sbslawsf.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: MITRA SATRAM VS. GROVE STREET MARKET ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Service Of Amended Summons And Second Amended Complaint On Defendant Mohammed Daqa
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday July 2, 2019, Line 7 DEFENDANT GROVE STREET MARKET'S Motion To Quash Service Of Amended Summons And Second Amended Complaint On Defendant Mohammed Daqa Defendant Mohammed Daqa's motion to quash service of amended summons and second amended complaint is granted. Service of summons may be quashed if service was improper and the Court therefore lacks jurisdiction of the defendant. (Code Civ. Proc. § 418.10; Fuss v. City of Los Angeles (1958) 162 Cal.App.2d 643, 646-647.) Although the Proof of Service submitted as Exhibit B to the Declaration of Ryan Whyte shows that the registered process server declares under penalty of perjury that he personally served Mr. Daqa on March 25, 2019 at 1:12 p.m., this merely creates a rebuttable presumption of proper service. (See Evid. Code § 647 ["The return of a process server registered pursuant to Chapter 16 (commencing with Section 22350) of Division 8 of the Business and Professions Code upon process or notice establishes a presumption, affecting the burden of producing evidence, of the facts stated in the return."].) This presumption may be rebutted by evidence that contradicts it. (City of Los Angeles v. Morgan (1951) 105 Cal.App.2d 726, 731.) The Declaration of Said Abdelmannan does just that by stating under penalty of perjury that, at the time of attempted service, Mr. Daqa was not present and was no longer employed by Defendant Grove Street Market. (Abdelmannan Decl. 5, 7.) Furthermore, Mr. Abdelmannan said that he had not seen Mr. Daqa for several months and believed he may be out of the country. (Id. 5.) Plaintiff Mitra Satram's one-page opposition does nothing to call into question Mr. Abdelmannan's credibility or the veracity of his statements. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ERIK STRAND VS. FILLMORE CAPITAL PARTNERS, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Cross Defendant Erik Strand Demurrer To Cross Complaint (Tentative Ruling Only Part 2 Of 2)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday July 2, 2019, Line 8, Cross Defendant Erik Strand Demurrer To Cross Complaint (Tentative Ruling Only Part 2 Of 2) Finally, this claim is akin to seeking an improper offset of employee wages. (See Barnhill v. Robert Saunders & Co. (1981) 125 Cal.App.3d 1, 6 ["We conclude that an employer is not entitled to a setoff of debts owing it by an employee against any wages due that employee."].) "A plaintiff may . . . not plead around an absolute bar to relief simply by recasting the cause of action as one for unfair competition." (Zhang v. Superior Court (2013) 57 Cal.4th 364, 377.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: ERIK STRAND VS. FILLMORE CAPITAL PARTNERS, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday July 2, 2019, Line 8, CROSS DEFENDANT ERIK STRAND DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT (part 1 of 2) Cross-Defendant Erik Strand's demurrer to Cross-Complainants Fillmore Capital Partners, LLC and Ronald E. Silva's cross-complaint is sustained without leave to amend. The sole cause of action for unfair business practices in violation of Business and Professions Code section 17200 et seq. fails because Cross-Complainants lack standing to bring such a claim. Standing to bring an Unfair Competition Law claim is limited to "any person who has suffered injury in fact and has lost money or property as a result of a violation of this chapter." (Bus. & Prof. Code § 17204; Kwikset Corp. v. Superior Court (2011) 51 Cal.4th 310, 321.) First, Cross-Complainants are unable to establish that they lost money "as a result" of allegedly fraudulent behavior. While Cross-Complainants allege that they paid Mr. Strand or his company "hundreds of thousands of dollars in fees" for IT services as a result of Mr. Strand's representations that he was an independent contractor and that they would not have continued to retain Mr. Strand had they known that he might actually be an employee, the parties' beliefs have little or nothing to do with the analysis of whether someone is an employee or an independent contractor. (Cross-Complaint 13, 16, 20; Santa Cruz Transp., Inc. v. Unemployment Ins. Appeals Bd. (1991) 235 Cal.App.3d 1363, 1372 [whether someone is an independent contractor is determined by the parties' conduct, not what the parties believe or say]; Toyota Motor Sales U.S.A. v. Superior Court (1990) 220 Cal.App.3d 864, 877 [same]; see Dynamex Operations West, Inc. v. Superior Court (2018) 4 Cal.5th 903, 955-957 [adopting three-factor "ABC" test for wage order claims]; S.G. Borello & Sons, Inc. v. Dept. of Indus. Relations (1989) 48 Cal.3d 341, 350 ["[t]he principal test of an employment relationship is whether the person to whom service is rendered has the right to control the manner and means of accomplishing the result desired."].) Nor could Mr. Strand reasonably have been expected to predict how a court would view the relationship between him and FCP, much less a change in law governing the distinction between employees and independent contractors. Thus, Mr. Strand's alleged representations have no bearing on whether he will be determined to be an employee as a matter of law and Cross-Complainants could not have reasonably relied on these alleged misrepresentations. Furthermore, Cross-Complainants fail to plead a concrete and particularized injury required for standing to bring a UCL claim. (Kwikset, 51 Cal.4th at 322.) They do not allege that they received less than they bargained for in retaining Mr. Strand, or that there was any future diminishment of the services Mr. Strand performed, or that Cross-Complainants were required to enter into any transaction that would have otherwise been unnecessary. (See id. at 323 [explaining examples of how economic injury from unfair competition may be shown].) Rather, the cross-complaint alleges that Mr. Strand performed the IT services and was paid for his work. (Cross-Complaint 12-13.) The only plausible economic harm is the potential future loss from a lawsuit if Mr. Strand is found to be an employee rather than an independent contractor. But Cross-Complainants do not claim such losses as damages and in any event such injury would be improperly hypothetical for purposes of standing. (See Kwikset, 51 Cal.4th at 322 [injury must be "actual or imminent, not conjectural or hypothetical"] [internal quotations omitted].) (end of part 1 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: MILTON BOONE BIRDSELL VS. ABM BUILDING SOLUTIONS, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Abm Building Solutions, Llc, Abm Industries Inc., Lee Lockridge, Bruce Wright, Tom Bowen'S Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Action (Tentative Ruling Only Part 2 Of 2)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday July 2, 2019, Line 11, Defendants Abm Building Solutions, Llc, Abm Industries Inc., Lee Lockridge, Bruce Wright, Tom Bowen'S Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Action (Tentative Ruling Only Part 2 Of 2) All causes of action are within the broad scope of the agreement and are arbitrable. The agreement is governed by the FAA by its express terms, and evidence also shows that it is an agreement involving interstate commerce. (Edwards Decl. 4; see Rodriguez v. American Technologies, Inc. (2006) 136 Cal.App.4th 1110, 1121-1122.) The FAA preempts Cal. Labor Code § 229. (Perry v. Thomas (1987) 482 U.S. 483, 492.) Finally, although there is a minimal degree of procedural unconscionability, because the arbitration agreement was a contract of adhesion, Mr. Birdsell has failed to show any substantive unconscionability. (See Pinnacle Museum Tower Assn. v. Pinnacle Market Development (US), LLC (2012) 55 Cal.4th 223, 247 ["Both procedural unconscionability and substantive unconscionability must be shown, but 'they need not be present in the same degree' and are evaluated on 'a sliding scale.'"].) The agreement is mutual and subjects all disputes between the employer and the employee to arbitration, without carving out any claims likely to be brought by the employer. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (end of part 2 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: MILTON BOONE BIRDSELL VS. ABM BUILDING SOLUTIONS, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Action
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday July 2, 2019, Line 11, DEFENDANTS ABM BUILDING SOLUTIONS, LLC, ABM INDUSTRIES INC., LEE LOCKRIDGE, BRUCE WRIGHT, TOM BOWEN'S Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Action (part 1 of 2) Defendants ABM Building Solutions, LLC, ABM Industries Inc., Lee Lockridge, Bruce Wright, and Tom Bowen's motion to compel arbitration of the claims asserted in plaintiff Milton Boone Birdsell's complaint is granted and the action is stayed pending conclusion of the arbitration proceedings. The court exercises its discretion to review evidence submitted with Defendants' reply brief. Plaintiff may respond at the hearing, including with submission of additional evidence. (Espejo v. Southern California Permanente Medical Group (2016) 246 Cal.App.4th 1047, 1056-1059; Alliant Ins. Services, Inc. v. Gaddy (2008) 159 Cal.App.4th 1292, 1308.) Defendants have met their burden to show that the 2015 Mutual Agreement to Arbitrate was executed by Mr. Birdsell as a condition of his employment. (Edwards Decl. 8). Although Mr. Birdsell does not recall signing the agreement, he does not dispute that the wet ink signature is his and does not assert that the signature was forged. (Birdsell Decl. 3). Thus, even without considering the evidence of other documents signed by Plaintiff during his employment, which Defendants adduced on reply to authenticate the wet ink signature, Defendants have met their burden of showing that Plaintiff executed the 2015 arbitration agreement. That is sufficient by itself to warrant granting the motion to compel. Defendants also met their burden to authenticate the electronic signature on the 2016 arbitration agreement. (Edwards Supp. Decl. 8-13; Edwards Decl. 9-10). (See Espejo v. Southern California Permanente Medical Group (2016) 246 Cal.App.4th 1047.) Defendants have shown that they are parties who may enforce the agreement, which covers ABM Facility Solutions Group, LLC and its subsidiary companies, as well as past, present, and future parents, subsidiaries, affiliates, successor entities, and employees. (Arbitration Agreement A; see also Nguyen v. Tran (2007) 157 Cal.App.4th 1032, 1036-1037.) ABM Facility Solutions Group, LLC was a wholly-owned subsidiary of Defendant ABM Industries Incorporated, while Defendant ABM Building Solutions, LLC was a wholly-owned subsidiary of the former ABM Facility Solutions Group, LLC, which in 2017 was merged into Defendant ABM Industries Incorporated. (Nelson Decl. 3). (end of part 1 of 2) =(302/EPS)
